speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven. Survivor is Evy Woods. The date is April 16, 1996. Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandparents, my mother's parents, at their engagement in 1912, taken in Obornik, at that time Germany, later Oborniki, Poland. And the person on the left is my grandmother, Regina, then [? Keschbaum, ?] that was her birth name. And he is Paul Loszinski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my other grandparents, and I'll start on the right. The right is my father, Ernst Morry Goldstein at age 12, so it's probably 1913, I believe. And he was the younger of two sons. The middle person is my grandmother, Tina Goldstein, and her maiden name was [? Mutek. ?] In fact, my two grandmothers were related. And on the right is my grandfather-- here we go again. I think it was [? Isador ?] Ignatz Goldstein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This-- this-- I did say 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was probably taken in Berlin, Germany. We spent a lot of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, as well as some of the others, came back from China, from Shanghai. My other grandmother's sister survived in Shanghai, her younger sister, with her son and husband. And she gave us a good number of these photographs because they had been in her possession when she went to China from Berlin. And of course, she knew we didn't have a shred of anything to our name, and that's how we gradually got some of these. I also want to mention that my great-grandmother is the same person on both sides of my grandparents' families. In other words, that would be sisters that married into the same family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an excerpt of a larger picture which had many people on it, which also came from my grandmother's sister. These are my mother and father the day they were married in Berlin, July 8, 1937. And at that time already, one could not have gold wedding rings, so she has this tarnished engraved ring that I have in my belongings. My mother is to the left, and her name was Herta Loszinski and now Goldstein, and this is my father, Ernst Morry Goldstein, who was 12 years older than my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me when-- in 1939. I was probably a year old, a little bit over a year, maybe 14 months. It was taken in Berlin. And again, it was my mother's aunt who had a number of photographs that she took with her to China and brought to San Francisco when we all met there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of two little sisters, whose father was some sort of an important Nazi, who had won some special decorations. And the children and the mother were staying in [INAUDIBLE], where we were, as I mentioned, in East Prussia, at that big, big castle, as it was called. And I'm the one on the right. The one in the middle is Heidi, and she's about three. That's the one with the black eyes and black hair. She should have been the Jewish child, according to her mother's degree. And the older one, I think, was a year older than I was, named [? Etmuta. ?] Sounds like a nice Nazi name to me, but they had no clue about anything. That was taken while we were living undercover in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of my mother taken in Vilna, while I was in the orphanage, as a matter of fact. Vilna, Lithuania, in some public park, if I remember right. And it's 1947, according to the back of the picture. My mother's wearing a pin, and you see the pin to the right of the picture, and we found that in a drawer in one of the camp places that we were. And for some reason unknown to either one of us, she picked it up, and the pin is still here. And she had some of that engraving redone to match my initials, EW. And so we're dragging the pin through life with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of, starting on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the left is a picture of my mother, Herta Goldstein then, still. And as you can see, she's looking pretty devastated, as if she's seen it all. We had just come back to Berlin in 1948, and this was in May of '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the right. My hair is just growing out from the shaved head business at the orphanage in Vilna, so I look a little sparse, a little thin, and I have the only pair of glasses on that there were in all of Lithuania. I don't know what my mother had to give for those, but I saved them until-- some years ago, I looked for them in my mother's belongings in San Francisco, and they've never turned up again-- gone. So it's all 1948, May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a picture taken in 1950 in Berlin, and I'm on the left-hand side, and on the right-hand side is my mother. We're looking a lot better by then, before emigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say both of your names again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and I'm Evy, at that time Evelyn, whatever set of names-- Thea Goldstein. I'm still having all those names. My mother is Herta Goldstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of-- and I have to call her by her full name-- Dr. Elizabeth Abegg, A-B-E-G-G. And it was taken-- I think it was her 75th birthday in Berlin, and she really was our primary rescuer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the '60s?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. She would be 100-and-yea-some. Yeah, but she was a most wonderful person you could imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of my other primary rescuer, so it must be secondary, Hilda [INAUDIBLE] Arnold and her daughter, [? Doila ?] which really means Dorothy. And this was taken in 1986 in Berlin. And you see there, to the right of the picture, at-- at Hilda's lapel, there's a funny little cross there. She had just received in-- in those weeks or days a humanitarian award, which is done in the form of a cross in Germany. But I ran afoul with the Yad Vashem honoring. Sent the check, sent the forms, sent the story, and Lord only knows what became of it because it didn't happen, so we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of our family. I guess you would say it's our new family, more or less. Seated and at the left, going from left to right, is my husband, John S. Woods. We've been married 24 years. I'm next to him, Evy Woods. See? I'm somebody else again. There's my mother, Herta Long. Long was her remarried name. She's going to be 82 in a couple of weeks. Next to her is little baby Grayson, who is meanwhile two years older than on the picture. It's Grayson Rudd, and his mother, who's my daughter, Sylvia Rudd. And then, up, standing, from the left again is John's daughter, Robyn Woods Pearson and her husband, Mike Pearson. They live in Idaho. And next to him is Mike Rudd, son-in-law-- we have two Mikes-- and Spencer, who is the oldest child of Sylvia and Mike, Spencer Rudd. This was taken here in Albuquerque at my mother's house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Evy, would you introduce your company that's sitting beside you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll start on the left here. This is my mother, Herta Long. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We've been through thick and thin, separately and together. And she's going to be 82 in two weeks, and she drives her own car and lives in her own house, and she's a tough lady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is my husband, John Woods, John S. Woods. And we've been together probably about 26 years that we've, more or less, been together. We've been married 24 years, and we raised his daughter and my daughter. And he's the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something to your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I would. She is my most priciest possession, my best friend, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and together with her husband, John, he is-- they are a team. I cannot describe-- describe them any better, really, I can't. And I'm very grateful for having the two of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aren't we all? And [INAUDIBLE], you're my most priciest possession also.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really didn't want to be a possession to anyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm proud of you both and love you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm sorry that my daughter Sylvia is not here. She's in Denver. I would like for her to have been able to be here, but it doesn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little short on notice, but she'll get to see all this, and so will her sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
